title: Is this the end of America's hegemony?
gist: Alan Wolff argues US hegemony is ending largely by self-inflicted choice, not external defeat, drawing a detailed historical parallel to Ancient Athens' decline (alliance overreach, wars of choice, internal instability) while predicting an eventual internationalist resurgence within US politics.

insights.claim: Alan Wolff argues that America's loss of hegemonic status, if occurring, is being caused by its own policy choices rather than by an external rival defeating it.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Frames US geopolitical risk as self-driven and policy-reversible rather than structurally inevitable, which matters for how investors weight the durability of dollar/alliance-based advantages.
insights.quote: if America's time as hegemon is ending, which certainly seems to be the case, it is due to its own actions, not from some external force.

insights.claim: Wolff frames Ancient Athens' fall from hegemony as a near-exact historical playbook for the US, except that Athens resisted its decline while America appears to be actively choosing this outcome.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Suggests the current US trajectory is a matter of deliberate political choice, implying policy reversal (not just external shocks) is the key variable to monitor for a change in course.
insights.quote: The historical record in the case of Athens offers a near playbook for America's loss of hegemonic status. Where it diverges is that America appears in key respects to be choosing this outcome whereas Athens resisted it.
insights.locator: opening comparison of Athens and America

insights.claim: The USSR's collapse in December 1991 removed America's last peer-level external challenger until China's more recent rise, meaning the current strain on US hegemony is a second, distinct competitive cycle.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Useful timeline marker: the ~30-year unipolar interval (1991-present) is treated by the author as the anomaly, not the baseline, when assessing how much US dominance investors should assume persists.

insights.claim: US dependence on imported oil rose from about 15 percent of total petroleum consumption in the early 1950s to 20 percent by the 1960s, mirroring Athens' dependence on imported grain from the Black Sea and Sicily as a structural vulnerability tied to hegemonic status.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Illustrates the recurring pattern that hegemons become dependent on secure trade routes for a critical commodity, a vulnerability that historically invites both military overextension and alliance friction.
insights.quote: By the early 1950s, the United States began relying on foreign sources of oil, with imports representing about 15 percent of total petroleum consumption, rising to 20 percent by the 1960s.

insights.claim: Both Athens and the US pursued broad multilateral institutions to lock in an open trading order after moments of conflict, but both efforts fell short of a single unifying political body: Pericles' 449 BCE Panhellenic congress was boycotted by Sparta, just as the US-backed United Nations failed to prevent an immediate postwar split with the USSR, leading instead to NATO and the GATT/WTO trade architecture.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Shows that hegemonic powers historically settle for parallel security and trade blocs rather than universal governance, a pattern relevant to assessing whether current fragmentation (WTO gridlock, NATO strain) is a repeat rather than a novel breakdown.
insights.locator: Athenian and American hegemonies, Panhellenic congress reference

insights.claim: Wolff draws a direct line from Athens forcing the Delian League into unequal trade terms, a common currency, and the Megarian Decree embargo to US actions insulting Canada, threatening Denmark with territorial acquisition over Greenland, demanding unequal 'Turnberry deals,' waging economic warfare via China export controls and tariffs, imposing tariffs broadly, and fighting a war in Iran its allies did not support.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Positions current tariff and territorial-pressure policies (Canada, Greenland, China tariffs) not as isolated headlines but as the specific mechanism that historically converts allies into resentful, war-prone rivals — a signal to watch allied trade/security realignment.
insights.quote: The United States has insulted its allies (e.g., Canada), threatened them with territorial acquisition (Denmark over Greenland), demanded unequal treaties (Turnberry deals), attempted economic warfare with its major opponent (China export controls and tariffs)
insights.locator: The parallels and sometimes near congruence of current US policy

insights.claim: Under Pericles, Athens required all Delian League allies to adopt Athenian currency, weights, and measures and imposed an embargo (the Megarian Decree) on a rival economy, behavior the article says set the stage for war with Sparta.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical mechanism (currency/standards coercion plus trade-monopoly sanctions) by which allied resentment escalates into open conflict, the template the author applies to current US tariff and sanctions policy.
insights.quote: Athens forced its allies into unequal trade agreements much resented by them. Under the Megarian Decree, it imposed an embargo against that city, using its trade monopoly to sanction a rival economy.
insights.locator: Athens' relations with its allies deteriorated

insights.claim: The United States annually spends more on its military than the next six countries combined (China, Russia, Germany, India, the United Kingdom, and Ukraine), and at times has exceeded even that.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Even amid declared hegemonic decline, hard military and dollar dominance remain quantitatively intact, so the erosion the author describes is about alliance trust and soft power, not raw capability, an important distinction for risk assessment.
insights.quote: the United States annually spends more on its military than the next six countries combined (China, Russia, Germany, India, the United Kingdom, and Ukraine)

insights.claim: The US Navy has historically been larger than all other navies in the world combined, underpinning America's post-1940s guarantee of open global sea lanes, just as Pericles' state-funded Athenian navy secured Athens' trade routes and deterred pirates.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Naval supremacy is the physical enabler of both hegemonies' trade security; any relative decline in this capability gap versus China would remove the material basis for the open-sea-lanes guarantee.
insights.quote: The US Navy was not just the largest in the world—it was larger than all other navies combined.

insights.claim: The Western alliance is holding together but showing strains, even as China, Russia, and North Korea pledge closer alignment among themselves, a bifurcation the author treats as a live geopolitical signal.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A tightening China-Russia-North Korea axis alongside a fraying Western bloc is the macro backdrop for sanctions effectiveness, supply-chain derisking, and defense-spending trends investors should track.
insights.quote: The Western alliance has held but is showing strains, while the autocratic powers of China, Russia, and North Korea, pledge closer alignment among themselves.

insights.claim: At a recent NATO summit, President Donald Trump reiterated his interest in the United States controlling Greenland, an act that caused renewed uneasiness among America's allies.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A sitting president raising territorial claims against a NATO ally (Denmark) at an alliance summit is cited as concrete evidence of the alliance-credibility erosion the article's thesis depends on.
insights.quote: Recently, at the NATO summit, President Donald Trump reiterated his interest in the United States controlling Greenland, causing renewed uneasiness among America's allies.
insights.locator: America's relations with its partners are still troubled

themes.theme: Internal erosion: universities, military leadership, and oligarchic influence

insights.claim: Historians such as Edward Gibbon and Bertrand Russell hold that the final blow to Athens' dominance came when Emperor Justinian I closed the city's schools run by non-Christian scholars in 529 AD, severely diminishing Athens' intellectual influence; Wolff parallels this with internal, administration-driven pressure on US universities today.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Positions attacks on university/research infrastructure as a soft-power decay mechanism with a historical precedent for being decisive, worth watching alongside cited evidence (Michael Clemens's research) that the US is already driving away international students at a long-term economic cost.
insights.quote: It is widely held that the final blow to Athens' dominant role occurred when the Roman emperor Justinian I closed the city's schools led by non-Christian scholars in 529 AD.

insights.claim: Over the past two years, senior US military leadership at the Pentagon has been repeatedly roiled by the removal of seasoned officers by civilian leadership, which Wolff likens to the Athenian removal of its chief general during the Sicilian Expedition.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Institutional churn in military command is flagged as one of the internal-instability markers historically associated with hegemonic decline, relevant to assessing execution risk in US foreign/defense policy.
insights.quote: The ranks of senior military officials here have been roiled by removal of seasoned military leaders by the civilian in control of the Pentagon during these last two years.
insights.locator: The eventual loss of Athenian hegemony

insights.claim: Wolff compares wealthy Americans' widely known efforts to influence public policy for their own benefit to the intermittent oligarchic coups that interrupted Athenian democracy, noting the American version has not involved seizing formal power outright.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Flags concentrated private wealth's policy influence as a structural echo of oligarchic instability in a declining hegemon, a softer but still notable data point for governance-risk analysis.
insights.quote: The richest of the richest Americans are equally widely known by name, and their interests in influencing public affairs and opinion are to a degree also well known.
insights.locator: The Athenian democracy was intermittently interrupted

themes.theme: Wars of choice and alliance credibility

insights.claim: Athens' Sicilian Expedition (413 BCE), a war of choice to conquer Syracuse, ended in catastrophic loss of manpower and ships; Wolff draws a parallel to America's war of choice in Iraq and, more recently, against Iran.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Wars of choice are cast as a recurring accelerant of hegemonic overextension; the Iran war's economic effects on US allies (noted elsewhere in the piece) are the modern analogue investors should watch for allied-relations fallout.
insights.quote: Athens launched a war of choice, the massive Sicilian Expedition to conquer Syracuse, which ended in a catastrophic loss of manpower and ships in 413 BCE.
insights.locator: History informs and provides a clearer understanding

insights.claim: Wolff argues that even costly wars of choice like Iraq and Iran matter less for US hegemony than the potential dismantling of alliances caused by casting doubt on the validity of US security pledges, specifically NATO's Article 5 mutual-defense guarantee.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Identifies credibility of the Article 5 commitment as the single most consequential variable for the durability of US-led security architecture, more important than military misadventures themselves — a key geopolitical tripwire to monitor.
insights.quote: These misadventures cannot rival however the potential dismantling of America's alliances through casting doubt on the validity of its pledges of support, such as the one contained in Article 5 of NATO.
insights.locator: History informs and provides a clearer understanding

themes.theme: Outlook: retreat now, possible resurgence later

insights.claim: Wolff states that America's partial retreat from the world seems irreversible, though he does not rule out an eventual American recovery of status, albeit in a different form and to a different extent than before.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Sets investor expectations that the current US retrenchment (tariffs, alliance friction, reduced global leadership role) should be treated as a durable regime shift rather than a temporary cyclical dip.
insights.quote: America's partial retreat from the world seems irreversible.

insights.claim: Wolff predicts that the internationalist wing of American politics, in the tradition of John F. Kennedy-era Democrats and George H.W. Bush-era Republicans, will regain prominence in reaction to the disappearance of the familiar, internationally engaged America of the post-WWII decades.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A contrarian-leaning call that current America First retrenchment is a phase, not the end state, suggesting policy risk (tariffs, alliance withdrawal) could reverse within a future political cycle rather than persist indefinitely.
insights.quote: If it does, and I think that it will, it will be built in part as a reaction to the disappearance during these last years of the America that was familiar
insights.locator: The next American leaders will face a problem

insights.claim: Wolff frames the current America First moment as a repeat of the 1930s dynamic Franklin Roosevelt and Secretary of State Cordell Hull confronted, implying today's leaders face an analogous task of eventually re-internationalizing US policy.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Provides a historical precedent (1930s America First giving way to post-WWII internationalism) for how long and how sharply US foreign policy pendulum swings can run, useful for scenario-planning policy reversal timing.
insights.locator: The next American leaders will face a problem
